file_name: Chu et al. - 2025 - Class-Agnostic Feature-Learning-Based Deep-Learning Model for Robust Melanoma Prediction.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a class-agnostic feature-learning-based deep-learning model for robust melanoma prediction. It utilized deep learning architectures such as ConvNeXt and ResNet, along with class-agnostic activation maps (CAAMs), to classify dermoscopic images of melanoma and nevus using the ISIC 2017 and 2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a class-agnostic feature-learning-based deep-learning model for melanoma prediction. It details the methodology using ConvNeXt and ResNet architectures, describes the use of ISIC 2017 and 2019 datasets, and reports new quantitative results including AUROC and Dice scores for robustness evaluation.

title: Class-Agnostic Feature-Learning-Based Deep-Learning Model for Robust Melanoma Prediction
journal: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
brief_summary: This study developed a robust deep-learning model for melanoma prediction using class-agnostic activation maps (CAAMs) to address inconsistencies in dermoscopic image analysis. The researchers utilized the ConvNeXt and ResNet architectures on the ISIC 2017 and 2019 datasets, which focused on distinguishing between melanoma and nevus lesions. Model performance was evaluated using the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) for classification accuracy and Dice scores for robustness and lesion recognition consistency. The proposed CAAM-based ConvNeXt model achieved an AUROC of 0.954 on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 0.843 on the ISIC 2017 dataset. Additionally, the method demonstrated improved robustness by maintaining consistent activation on lesions despite image transformations and artifacts, outperforming baseline models in Dice score evaluations.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for prediction and classification in the study. Used in ISIC 2017 (374 training, 30 validation, 117 test cases) and ISIC 2019 (3,699 training, 267 validation, 505 test cases) datasets.
Comparator class for distinguishing from melanoma. Used in ISIC 2017 (1,372 training, 78 validation, 393 test cases) and ISIC 2019 (10,376 training, 733 validation, 1,495 test cases) datasets.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and nevus categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a deep-learning model for melanoma prediction, specifically distinguishing between melanoma and nevus classes using the ISIC 2017 and 2019 datasets. Performance was evaluated using AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and accuracy. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (melanoma vs. nevus).

all_task_types.target: Weakly supervised lesion segmentation masks derived from Class-Agnostic Activation Maps (CAAMs).
all_task_types.details: The study investigated the possibility of weakly supervised learning for lesion segmentation by comparing CAAMs with ground-truth segmentation masks from the ISIC 2017 dataset. Dice scores and Hausdorff distances were used to evaluate the consistency between the generated CAAMs and the actual lesion segmentation masks, but this was an analysis of the classification model's feature focus rather than a primary segmentation task output.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available collection of dermoscopic skin lesion images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, annotated by experienced dermatologists. The study focused on melanoma and nevus classes.

use.training: Used for training the deep-learning models (ResNet and ConvNeXt).
use.validation: Used for validation during model development.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (AUROC, Dice scores) and robustness evaluation.
use.other: Used for weakly supervised lesion segmentation analysis by comparing CAAMs with ground-truth segmentation masks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available collection of dermoscopic skin lesion images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. Since an official test set was not available, the original training and validation sets were combined and re-split.

use.training: Used for training the deep-learning models (ResNet and ConvNeXt) after re-splitting the original combined training/validation data.
use.validation: Used for validation during model development.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (AUROC, Dice scores) and robustness evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Severance Hospital Clinical Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset comprising dermoscopic images collected from the Department of Dermatology, Severance Hospital, Seoul, Korea, used to validate the generalizability of the proposed method.

use.testing: Used for external validation to assess robustness and generalization to real clinical images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To present all statistical results (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, accuracy) derived from five replicates.
To generate the statistical results and confidence intervals for model performance evaluation.

To quantitatively evaluate the robustness of the model by calculating the difference between the transformed Class-Agnostic Activation Map (CAAM) of the original image and the CAAM of the transformed image, and to compare CAAMs with ground-truth segmentation masks.

To evaluate the consistency between generated CAAMs and actual lesion segmentation masks (mentioned in summary/metrics list, though specific values not detailed in provided chunks, it is listed as a metric used).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Model performance was evaluated using AUROC, Dice scores, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and accuracy, with results presented as means with 95% confidence intervals derived from five replicates.

ConvNeXt-Tiny.notes: Best performance among all models on this dataset.

ResNet-50.notes: Performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, F1, accuracy) summarized in Table I, but specific values not explicitly listed in the text chunks. Baseline model showed poorest performance.

robustness_Dice_score.notes: Dice scores calculated between transformed CAAM of original image and CAAM of transformed image. Proposed model exhibited best AUROC performance after transformation.

ConvNeXt-Tiny.notes: Best performance among all models on this dataset.

ResNet-50.notes: Performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, F1, accuracy) summarized in Table III, but specific values not explicitly listed in the text chunks. Baseline model showed poorest performance.

robustness_Dice_score.notes: Dice scores calculated between transformed CAAM of original image and CAAM of transformed image. Proposed model exhibited best AUROC performance after transformation.

weakly_supervised_segmentation_consisten.notes: Comparison of CAAMs with ground-truth segmentation masks from ISIC 2017 dataset.

Severance Hospital Clinical Dataset.notes: External validation dataset comprising 714 dermoscopic images. Proposed model demonstrated superior AUROC compared to conventional model.

ResNet-50.mechanism: Deep residual learning with skip connections
ResNet-50.pooling: Adaptive average pooling to 2048-dimensional feature vector

ConvNeXt-Tiny.type: Modern CNN inspired by Transformer training strategies
key_innovations: Depthwise convolution (operates on each channel independently)
Inverted bottleneck design (input channels expanded to higher-dimensional space then projected back)
ConvNeXt-Tiny.pooling: Adaptive average pooling to 768-dimensional feature vector

training_details.hardware: Intel i9-13900K processor, 64 GB DDR4 RAM, 1 TB SSD, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090TI (24 GB GPU)
loss_functions: LCAAM (Mean square error loss between transformed CAAM of original image and CAAM of transformed image)
data_augmentation: Color jitter (brightness: 0.8-1.2, contrast: 0.8-1.2, saturation: 0.8-1.2, hue: -0.1 to 0.1)
training_details.weight_management: Separately managed weights for feature extractor and classifier following global average pooling

classification_or_output_head.details: Uses weights associated with class nodes to generate Class Activation Maps (CAMs) or Class-Agnostic Activation Maps (CAAMs). CAAM is produced by summing feature maps across the channel axis and normalizing.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation during training: random horizontal and vertical flips, random square crop (160 to 210 pixels), shear (angle range: -40 to 40), elastic transformation (alpha: 0 to 40, beta: 1 to 5), and color jitter (brightness: 0.8-1.2, contrast: 0.8-1.2, saturation: 0.8-1.2, hue: -0.1 to 0.1)
Image transformation for robustness evaluation: shear, elastic, and color jitter transformations applied to test images to assess model consistency
Normalization: Class-Agnostic Activation Maps (CAAMs) normalized by subtracting minimum value and dividing by range (aimax - aimin)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as baseline transformation methods for training.
Range of 160 to 210 pixels, chosen to ensure lesions remained intact and were not cropped out.
Alpha range: 0 to 40; Beta range: 1 to 5.
Brightness: 0.8 to 1.2; Contrast: 0.8 to 1.2; Saturation: 0.8 to 1.2; Hue: -0.1 to 0.1.
Input images were resized to 224 x 224 pixels after transformation.

conclusions: The proposed class-agnostic feature-learning-based deep-learning model using ConvNeXt-Tiny achieved superior classification performance compared to ResNet-50 and baseline models, with an AUROC of 0.954 on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 0.843 on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

The method demonstrated enhanced robustness to image transformations (shear, elastic, and color jitter) by maintaining consistent Class-Agnostic Activation Maps (CAAMs) on lesions, as evidenced by higher Dice scores (0.664 for ConvNeXt on ISIC 2019 and 0.557 on ISIC 2017) compared to baseline models.

The proposed model effectively focused on lesion boundaries and color variations while ignoring common dermoscopic artifacts such as ruler markers, skin hair, and ink markers, unlike baseline models which showed inconsistent activation on these artifacts.
The model generalized well to real-world clinical settings, achieving an AUROC of 0.946 on the external Severance Hospital clinical dataset compared to 0.921 for the conventional model.

The analysis of CAAMs against ground-truth segmentation masks from the ISIC 2017 dataset revealed greater consistency with actual lesion areas, suggesting the potential for weakly supervised lesion segmentation using only classification labels.

ConvNeXt-Tiny proved more suitable than ResNet-50 for CAAM-based losses due to its ability to more clearly identify global features within images, resulting in more precise and global activation across lesions.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification of melanoma and nevus, excluding other skin lesion classes present in the ISIC datasets, which limits direct comparison with broader multi-class studies.

The data transformations used for robustness evaluation (shear, elastic, color jitter) are relatively simple and may not fully capture the complexities and diversity of real-world skin-lesion variations.

The ISIC 2019 dataset did not have an official test set; the authors combined the original training and validation sets and re-split them, which may introduce data leakage or bias compared to using a held-out test set.

The external validation was performed on a single private clinical dataset from Severance Hospital in Seoul, Korea, which may limit the generalizability of the results to other geographic populations or clinical settings.

The proposed model's reliance on Class-Agnostic Activation Maps (CAAMs) for robustness is a novel approach, but the consistency between CAAMs and ground-truth segmentation masks was moderate (Dice score 0.481), suggesting that weakly supervised segmentation via this method is not yet perfect.
The code for the proposed method is publicly available on GitHub, which supports reproducibility.
